cards.front: Configure OSPF process 1 to advertise 10.1.1.0/24 in area 0.
cards.back:
```
router ospf 1
 network 10.1.1.0 0.0.0.255 area 0
 router-id 1.1.1.1
```
The `1` after `router ospf` is the local process ID — locally significant, doesn't have to match neighbors.

cards.front: Why does the OSPF `network` command use a wildcard mask?
cards.back: It selects which router INTERFACES will run OSPF, by IP. Wildcard 0 = exact match, 0.0.0.255 = any 4th-octet value, 0.0.255.255 = any 3rd/4th octet, etc. The wildcard 0.0.0.0 with the interface IP exactly matches just that one interface.

cards.front: Alternative to `network`: per-interface OSPF activation. Show syntax.
cards.back:
```
interface gi0/0
 ip ospf 1 area 0
```
More explicit and modern. Used alongside or instead of `network` statements. Each interface gets `ip ospf <process> area <area>`.

cards.front: What 3 commands verify basic OSPF operation?
cards.back: `show ip ospf neighbor` — neighbor list, state, DR/BDR. `show ip route ospf` — OSPF-learned routes. `show ip ospf interface brief` — which interfaces run OSPF, area, cost, neighbor count. Add `show ip protocols` for process summary.

cards.front: What is a passive OSPF interface and when do you use it?
cards.back: An OSPF-enabled interface that does NOT send/receive hellos, so it forms no adjacencies, but its subnet IS still advertised. Used on LAN interfaces facing end users (no other routers there). Configure: `passive-interface <int>` or `passive-interface default` then `no passive-interface <int>` per active link.

cards.front: How is OSPF cost calculated by default, and how do you tune it?
cards.back: Cost = reference-bandwidth (default 100 Mbps) ÷ interface bandwidth. Anything ≥ 100 Mbps gets cost 1 — which loses fidelity for gigabit/10Gig links. Best practice: raise reference-bandwidth: `auto-cost reference-bandwidth 100000` (100 Gbps). Per-interface override: `ip ospf cost <1-65535>`.

cards.front: What is the OSPF default hello interval, and how do you change it?
cards.back: 10 seconds (broadcast/PtP) or 30 seconds (NBMA). Change per interface: `ip ospf hello-interval <seconds>`. Dead interval automatically becomes 4× hello (or set explicitly with `ip ospf dead-interval <seconds>`).

cards.front: OSPFv2 vs OSPFv3 — what's the difference, and what does each support?
cards.back: OSPFv2 (RFC 2328): IPv4. OSPFv3 (RFC 5340): originally IPv6, later extended to support both IPv4 and IPv6 via address families. OSPFv3 uses link-local IPv6 source addresses for hellos, drops authentication from the protocol itself (uses IPsec), and routes are per-link rather than per-subnet.

cards.front: OSPF router roles: Internal, Backbone, ABR, ASBR — what is each?
cards.back: Internal router: all interfaces in a single non-zero area. Backbone router: at least one interface in area 0. ABR (Area Border Router): interfaces in two or more areas (one usually being area 0). ASBR (Autonomous System Boundary Router): redistributes routes from outside the OSPF domain (static, BGP, EIGRP) into OSPF. A single router can be more than one type.

cards.front: OSPF route types: intra-area (O), inter-area (O IA), external (O E1 / O E2) — what does each mean?
cards.back: Intra-area (O): destination is in the SAME area as the receiving router; computed via SPF on the local LSDB. Inter-area (O IA): destination is in a DIFFERENT area, learned via Type-3 LSAs from an ABR. External (O E1, O E2): destination is OUTSIDE the OSPF AS, redistributed by an ASBR. E1 = metric increases at each hop; E2 (default) = metric is fixed at the value the ASBR advertised, no matter how far you travel.

cards.front: Does the OSPF process ID need to match between neighbors? Does single-area OSPF need to use area 0?
cards.back: Process ID: NO — locally significant only (just like EIGRP AS number is required to match, but OSPF's process-id is not). Single-area OSPF: NO — you can use any area number, but if multi-area is added later, area 0 must exist. Best practice: use area 0 even for single-area to avoid future re-design.

cards.front: How long do OSPF LSAs live before being refreshed/aged out?
cards.back: Default LSA aging timer = 30 minutes (1800 s). The originating router refreshes its LSAs every 30 minutes by re-flooding them with a higher sequence number. If an LSA reaches MaxAge (3600 s = 1 hour) without a refresh, it's flushed from the LSDB.
